author: @clive280178
text: Hi Robert got a question about induction after seeing something which clamps around power lines and harvests the electromagnetic field supplied by the lines. Does tapping into the magnetic field with another device add a load on the original source or is it like a byproduct which is wasted which could be used? Many thanks

replies.author: @hanslepoeter5167
replies.text: Hi Clive. If I may answer that it does add a load to the original source. When you do that it's just a transformer. Even if it's just a single wire, It's the same principle. When putting a load on a transformer secundary winding certainly the load primary increases. So it does ...

replies.author: @clive280178
replies.text: @hanslepoeter5167 hi Hans many thanks I thought it would do, how about putting some magnets near it would they rotate (if on a rotor shaft) or be energised/move without adding a load or is it essentially the same thing as putting a device/appliance with a power rating on. A DC motor can turn at full speed using very little amperage. Yes I live right underneath some power lines haha

replies.author: @hanslepoeter5167
replies.text: @clive280178 Magnets could rotate in theory but whatever you do extracting power from it it is a load on the power line. You will not be able to tap any serious power without connecting to it  directly which I certainly recommend against. It will be illegal and maybe lethal too.

replies.author: @clive280178
replies.text: @hanslepoeter5167many thanks for the info

author: @maintoc
text: Good / interesting / potentially useful video.  ~  Re:  2:46  "... the North follows the North and the South follows the South."   Considering that the North-pointing pole of the compass needle is a North-seeking pole, that means that it's actually the South pole of the compass needle, yes?  As it's opposite poles that attract each other, it can't be "... the North follows the North and the South follows the South."  Correct?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: the compass north points to magnetic north mate - the compass north is really a south painted with a red north -  it would be terribly confusing if you were in the woods and you had to remember that! - so the paint the South Pole with a red N

replies.author: @maintoc
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Agreed, but for you to say that "... the North follows the North and the South follows the South."  is technically incorrect in terms of what's going on regarding the polarities involved.

author: @dremaboy777
text:
'This' is the configuration I was describing when you had unfavourable results with that large diameter single coil; in attempt to replicate the microwave turntable motor coil, to a generator
Those top and bottom plates that encase the coil. In the center of the coil, the metal arms come down.

But, this video was very profitable. It answers the question I was posing on that video. Now I know the single coil, multi-pole concept generator design ... will work.

👍

replies.author: @AtlasReburdened
replies.text: Magnets aren't a source of energy. They seem mysterious because they can do things like keep an object suspended with just their field, but so can hooks, and no one is trying to make free energy from hooks, in spite of the fact that what gives a hook it's rigidity is a multitude of fields acting between its atoms.
